insights.insight: Ad tech charges $5–$150 CPA for probabilistic attribution; this protocol settles verified purchase proof at roughly $0.10 per receipt — a 50–200x cost difference that isn't a pricing negotiation, it's a different category of signal.

Cookies are dead, device fingerprinting is degrading, and first-party data stops at your own ecosystem boundary; verified receipt data crosses that boundary without identity attachment, which no current ad tech stack produces.

insights.topic: Rural coverage as a data moat
insights.insight: 17 active CBSAs with a demonstrated rural spine across 11+ non-metro state designations — ad tech datasets are structurally metro-biased because they follow ad spend density, so this geographic distribution is genuinely differentiated, not just larger.

One active predicate — a confirmed brand querying verified purchase history — is a thin but real signal that buy-side demand exists; the dataset is being tested against real commercial intent before scale has arrived.

Physical retail (grocery, home improvement, club) and SaaS/dev infrastructure receipts are on a collision course in the same dataset; if five pending SaaS vendor requests activate, this becomes the first queryable layer combining verified physical and digital commerce spend.

Expansion is arriving in discrete geographic pulses across unconnected markets, not metro clusters — that pattern fingerprints social graph spread, not paid acquisition, which means the supply side is building without marketing costs distorting the data's representativeness.
